summary: Justin Brennan conducts a multifamily mentorship Q&A covering due diligence processes, LLC structuring for non-syndicated deals, inspection protocols, and pitch deck calculations for investor presentations. The session addresses practical underwriting questions and entity formation strategy relevant to value-add multifamily acquisitions.
takeaways: Segregate deferred maintenance from capital improvements during inspections—deferred maintenance is negotiable for price reduction, improvements generate additional rent
For unit-by-unit inspections on 12-15 unit properties: allocate 1-2 inspectors for 1 day, plus separate professional plumber with camera for sewer scope
Big ticket items requiring negotiation: mold, health/safety issues, foundations, structure, roofing, water heaters—don't nitpick minor appliances on older buildings (1970s+)
Closing costs should total ~6% of purchase price and cover acquisition fee (2-3%), title, escrow, and 1% lender fee
For non-syndicated deals, create state-specific LLC (assignable contract required) in last 10 days of due diligence once financials and inspections validate deal viability
DSCR loans attribute to entity EIN only, not personal credit—lenders pull personal credit as fraud check, not for qualification; balance sheet equal to loan amount required if entity looks weak

Pitch deck investor example calculations: for $100,000 investor, divide their investment by total equity raised to get percentage (e.g., $100K ÷ $1M total = 10%), then apply cash-on-cash and total return percentages to that investor's specific amount
Minimum investment threshold for 506(b) syndication offerings: $25,000 (accredited/non-accredited mix)—initial raise typically $25K, $50K, $75K, $100K tranches rather than single $100K minimum

skill_candidates.change: Develop systematic inspection categorization separating deferred maintenance from capital improvements to optimize negotiation leverage
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin instructs: 'You're going to segregate things from improvements and deferred maintenance...what is a deferred maintenance column versus what is an improvement? Meaning if I improve this, I'm gonna get more rent...If I fix this, am I gonna get any more rent? The answer is no...big ticket items, meaning if they're dealing with mold, if you're dealing with health and safety, foundations, structure, roofing, big ticket items, water heaters...you want to focus on those and not nitpick smaller stuff'

skill_candidates.change: Establish concrete underwriting rules for closing cost reserves and loan structure modeling
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin teaches: '6% should cover just about all your closing cost. It's going to cover your acquisition fee, title, escrow, uh a 1% lender fee, stuff like that' and explains dropdown functionality in deal analyzer for including/excluding renovation budget from loan amounts to properly model equity impact

skill_candidates.change: Apply precise pitch deck waterfall calculations tied to specific investor check sizes rather than aggregate LP returns
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin demonstrates: 'to figure out the percentage for say a $100,000 investor that goes on the pitch deck, you would take 100,000 divided by the total equity. Let's say it was a million dollars. That's 10%, right?' and instructs to calculate cash-on-cash and total return percentages only for the specific investor amount, not total LP pool

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Multifamily Due Diligence Tips_09-30-25 | Justin Brennan
